title: Tuli's Whistle
author: Fablewick
lesson: The hard part is in the middle. Keep going anyway.
summary: Tuli has decided to learn how to whistle. It is not going well.
coverIllustration: <svg viewBox='0 0 200 150' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><ellipse cx='100' cy='110' rx='35' ry='25' fill='#D4925E'/><path d='M75 90 L65 70 L80 85 Z' fill='#D4925E'/><path d='M125 90 L135 70 L120 85 Z' fill='#D4925E'/><circle cx='90' cy='100' r='3' fill='#2A2118'/><circle cx='110' cy='100' r='3' fill='#2A2118'/><ellipse cx='100' cy='115' rx='3' ry='2' fill='#2A2118'/><circle cx='100' cy='118' r='4' fill='none' stroke='#2A2118' stroke-width='2'/><path d='M140 60 Q150 55 160 65 M150 75 Q160 70 168 80' stroke='#B26F3D' stroke-width='2' fill='none' opacity='0.6'/></svg>

1.label: Little Listener
1.text:
Tuli the fox heard a shepherd whistle.

One bright note, far across the field.

Tuli wanted a whistle of his own.

Tuli made a little O with his mouth.

Tuli blew.

"PFFFF."

No whistle. Not yet.

Tuli blew again.

"PFFFFFFF."

No whistle. Not yet.

A squirrel watched from a branch.

"You are not whistling," said the squirrel.

"I know," said Tuli.

Tuli tried all morning.

Pfff. Pfff. Pfff.

Tuli's mouth got tired.

Tuli flopped down with a thump.

"Maybe I cannot whistle," said Tuli.

"Maybe," said the squirrel. "Or maybe you can. Just not yet."

Not yet, thought Tuli.

Tuli took a big breath.

Tuli made the little O.

Tuli blew.

"Pfff."

Nothing.

One more try.

"PFEEEE!"

A whistle! A tiny one. A wobbly one.

But a whistle.

"Did you hear that?" said Tuli.

"I heard it!" said the squirrel, hopping up and down.

The whistle was small.

The whistle was short.

But it was Tuli's.

"I am going to do that again," said Tuli.

And he did. The next day. And the next.

Little by little, the whistle grew.

2.label: New Reader
2.text:
Tuli the young fox had made up his mind: today, he would learn to whistle.

He had heard a shepherd whistle one morning: one clean, bright note that sailed across the whole field, and a dog came running from far, far away. Tuli had been thinking about it ever since. A whistle could call your friends. A whistle could say hello. A whistle was a song that fit inside your mouth. Tuli wanted one very much.

So Tuli found a sunny patch of moss. He made a careful little O with his mouth, just like the shepherd. He took a breath. He blew.

"PFFFFFF," went Tuli.

No whistle.

Tuli frowned. He tried again.

"PFFFFFFFFFF."

No whistle.

A squirrel watched from a low branch, nibbling an acorn very, very slowly, the way squirrels do when they have found a good show.

"You are not whistling," said the squirrel.

"I know," said Tuli. And he blew again.

He tried all morning. Pff. Pfff. Pffffff. Sometimes a little hiss came out. Sometimes nothing came out at all. Once his lips made a buzzy, flappy flutter. Not a whistle, but at least a brand-new sound.

By lunch, Tuli's mouth was tired. By afternoon, it was very tired. Tuli flopped onto the moss with a thump.

"Maybe," he said slowly, "I just cannot whistle."

The squirrel stopped mid-acorn.

"Maybe," said the squirrel. "Or maybe you can. Just not yet. Cannot and cannot-yet are different things. Most things start as cannot-yet. Then, if you keep going, they turn into can. The middle part is the hard part. The middle part is where most foxes give up."

Tuli thought about that for a long minute. The middle part. He was definitely in the middle part.

He sat up. He took a careful breath. He made the careful O.

"Pff."

Nothing.

He did not stop. He tried again.

And then —

"PFEEEE."

A whistle. A tiny, thin, wobbly whistle. But a whistle, sure as sunlight.

Tuli froze. The squirrel froze.

"DID YOU HEAR THAT?" shouted Tuli, so loudly the whistle ran away again.

"I heard it!" said the squirrel, hopping straight up off the branch.

The whistle had been small. It had not lasted long. It was more squeak than song.

But Tuli had whistled. And now he knew, really and truly knew, the way you only know after the first time, that he could.

"I am going to do that again," said Tuli, very seriously.

And he did. Every day. Little by little, it got better.

3.label: On Your Own
3.text:
Tuli the young fox had made up his mind. He had decided it at sunrise, with the particular firmness of a creature too young to know better and too determined to be talked out of anything: today, Tuli was going to learn to whistle.

It had started with the shepherd.

The day before, Tuli had been watching a shepherd from a safe distance (foxes always watch shepherds from a safe distance) when the shepherd whistled. Just once. One clean, bright, two-note whistle that sailed across the whole field, and a dog came running from very far away. The sound landed in Tuli's chest and stayed there. A whistle, thought Tuli. A whistle was a tool. A whistle was a song that fit inside your mouth. A whistle could call your friends from across a valley. Tuli wanted one very, very much.

So this morning he found a sunny patch of moss in the clearing, sat down with great seriousness, and began.

He made a careful little O with his mouth, just the way the shepherd had. He took in a slow, measured breath. He blew.

"PFFFFFFF," went Tuli.

No whistle.

Tuli frowned. He adjusted the O. He tried again.

"PFFFFFFFFFF."

No whistle.

A squirrel had appeared on a nearby oak branch with an acorn, and was nibbling it very, very slowly, the way you eat when you have stumbled into excellent free entertainment.

"You are not whistling," the squirrel offered, after one especially long PFFFF.

"I had noticed," said Tuli, and blew again.

He worked at it all morning. Pff. Pfff. Pffff. He tried tight lips. He tried loose lips. He tried tilting his head, then not tilting his head. Once his lips made a flapping, buzzing flutter that was not a whistle but was, at least, a sound they had never made before, and for a moment Tuli felt almost proud of it.

By late morning his mouth was tired. By midday it was exhausted. By afternoon it felt like it had been chewing one very tough leaf for several hours.

Tuli slumped onto the moss with a thump. Ears down. Tail flat. Chin on paws.

"Maybe," he said slowly, mostly to himself, "I just cannot whistle. Maybe foxes don't whistle. Maybe my mouth is the wrong shape. Maybe —"

The squirrel set down its acorn and leaned along the branch.

"Maybe you cannot whistle," said the squirrel. "Or maybe you can. Just not yet. Those are not the same thing."

"They feel like the same thing," said Tuli, into the moss.

"They are not," said the squirrel. "Cannot is a wall. Cannot-yet is a door you haven't opened. Most things in the world start out as cannot-yet. Walking. Climbing. Counting. Catching fish out of a stream — I fell in a great many times before I sorted that one out, and I am, technically, a creature who is not supposed to like water."

Tuli lifted his chin a little. "What was the hard part?"

"The middle," said the squirrel, without even pausing to think. "The beginning is exciting: a brand-new thing! The end is wonderful: you can do the thing! But the middle is boring, and tiring, and it does not feel like you are getting anywhere at all. The middle is where most foxes give up. The middle is where you have to remember: boring and tiring are not the same as not working. You are working. You just can't see it yet."

Tuli thought about that. The middle. He was definitely in the middle.

He sat up. He brushed the moss off his chest. He took a careful breath and made the careful O.

"Pff."

Nothing.

He did not stop.

He tried again.

And then —

"PFEEEEE."

A whistle. A small whistle. A thin, wobbly, slightly surprised-sounding whistle. But unmistakably, clearly, completely, undeniably, a whistle.

Tuli froze.

The squirrel froze, acorn halfway to its mouth.

"DID YOU HEAR THAT," said Tuli, in a voice so loud and joyful that the whistle instantly fled.

"I HEARD IT," said the squirrel, hopping straight up off its branch.

The whistle had been small. The whistle had been short. It had been, honestly, more of a squeak than the clean two-note thing the shepherd had sent across the field. It had lasted, at best, half a second.

But Tuli had whistled.

And he knew now, really and truly, all the way down in his bones, that he could.

"I am going to do that again," said Tuli, very seriously.

And he did. Not right away. The rest of that afternoon was mostly pff. But the next day, twice. The day after that, four times. And by the end of the week, when the shepherd's bright two-note whistle came sailing across the field, a small, clean, slightly proud whistle sailed right back from the woods.
